codeReview.summary: Commit 7adb982 adds the wiring code for SecretScanner::scan_staged() inside git_commit(), but the wiring is incomplete: GitTool::new() initializes secret_scanner to None and no production code path calls GitTool::with_secret_scanner(). As a result, the scan_staged() call is dead code in practice — secret_scanner is always None when GitTool is used.

issues.description: GitTool::new() initializes secret_scanner to None (line 521), and with_secret_scanner() is never called by any production caller found in the codebase. The scan_staged() call inside git_commit() (lines 823-840) will never execute because self.secret_scanner is always None. The prior blocking issue ('git_commit function in tools.rs does NOT call SecretScanner::scan_staged() before committing') is only partially addressed — the call exists but is unreachable in practice.

The scan_staged() invocation is inside `if let Some(ref scanner) = self.secret_scanner { ... }`, which means scanning only happens if a scanner was explicitly injected. Since no caller injects one, this branch is never taken. The feature description requires 'Wire secret_scanning.rs to run Gitleaks-compatible regex patterns against staged file content before git commit operations' — this is not enforced at the point of tool execution.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The same pattern from safety-credential-proxy reappears: a security component is implemented with a builder/builder-pattern for optional wiring, but no actual caller injects it. The synthesis.json already flagged this as systemic ('Workers incorrectly declared features complete when only unit tests passed without verifying actual integration'). The documentation does not clarify what 'wired' means for middleware-style integrations.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Grep across crates/swell-tools and crates/swell-orchestrator shows no calls to with_secret_scanner(). Only GitTool::new() exists in production code. Compare safety-credential-proxy.json blocking issue: 'GitTool (in tools.rs) does not use CredentialProxy' — same pattern applies here.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The features.json specifies skillName 'rust-worker' but this skill does not exist at .factory/skills/rust-worker/. Transcript from prior round shows worker received 'No such file or directory' when trying to load the skill. Workers proceed without skill guidance.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript from 8d6e6cf1 shows: 'cat: /Users/ludwigjonsson/.factory/skills/rust-worker/SKILL.md: No such file or directory'. Prior review safety-credential-proxy.json also notes this skill gap.

summary: The fix adds the scan_staged() call inside git_commit() and the with_secret_scanner() builder, which is correct architecture. However, the builder is never called in any production code path — every GitTool::new() creates an instance with secret_scanner: None, making the scan unreachable. The feature cannot fulfill its security contract ('Commit is blocked when secrets are detected') because the scanner is never actually injected when GitTool is used. This is the same wiring gap identified in safety-credential-proxy. The scan logic itself (in secret_scanning.rs) is solid; the issue is purely at the integration point where GitTool should be created with a scanner.
